note.text: Longstanding adult-onset asthma. History of variable adherence to daily controller therapy.

coding.display: budesonide 0.09 MG/ACTUAT Dry Powder Inhaler [Pulmicort Flexhaler]

dosageInstruction.text: Inhale 2 puffs by mouth twice daily

medicationCodeableConcept.text: Albuterol HFA 90 mcg - rescue inhaler, PRN

dosageInstruction.text: Inhale 2 puffs by mouth every 4-6 hours as needed for wheezing or shortness of breath

resource.conclusion: Spirometry within normal limits. FEV1 3.6L (100% baseline), FEV1/FVC 82%. No airflow obstruction at this visit.
No acute cardiopulmonary process. Lungs mildly hyperinflated. No focal infiltrate, effusion, or pneumothorax. Findings compatible with reactive airway disease; no evidence of pneumonia.

coding.display: CBC panel - Blood by Automated count

resource.conclusion: CBC within normal limits. Mildly elevated total IgE (145 IU/mL) consistent with mild allergic component; eosinophils within normal range.

text.div: Patient reports asthma well-controlled on current regimen. Denies nocturnal symptoms, rescue inhaler use averages less than twice weekly. No recent ED visits or hospitalizations. Never smoker.

VS: BP 118/76, HR 72, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, Temp 36.8C. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheeze. Spirometry: FEV1 3.6L, FVC 4.4L, FEV1/FVC 82% (normal). PEF 480 L/min. ACT score 23 (well controlled). CXR: no acute process, mild hyperinflation.
Asthma, moderate persistent, well controlled on ICS monotherapy.
Continue budesonide (Pulmicort) 90mcg 2 puffs BID. Continue albeuterol PRN. Routine follow-up in 6-12 months, sooner if symptoms worsen. Reviewed inhaler technique.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 4 tablets (40 mg) by mouth once daily for 5 days

coding.display: C reactive protein [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

text.div: Patient presented with 2 days of progressive dyspnea, wheeze, and cough after an upper respiratory infection. Reports increased rescue inhaler use with minimal relief.
VS: BP 132/84, HR 112, RR 26, SpO2 91% RA, Temp 37.1C. Diffuse expiratory wheeze bilaterally, accessory muscle use. CRP 8.4 mg/L (elevated).
Acute moderate-severe asthma exacerbation, likely viral trigger.
Nebulized albuterol/ipratropium, supplemental O2, oral prednisone 40mg daily x5 days. Discharged after improvement to SpO2 96% RA. Instructed to follow up with pulmonology in 3 days.

coding.display: budesonide 0.08 MG/ACTUAT / formoterol fumarate 0.0045 MG/ACTUAT Metered Dose Inhaler [Symbicort]

dosageInstruction.text: Inhale 2 puffs by mouth twice daily

note.text: Stepped up from budesonide monotherapy after ED visit for exacerbation; discontinue prior ICS-only inhaler.

resource.conclusion: Mild-to-moderate airflow obstruction, FEV1 down 19% from baseline (3.6L to 2.9L). Consistent with recent exacerbation; recommend step-up therapy.

text.div: Follow-up after ED visit 3 days ago for asthma exacerbation. Reports residual mild dyspnea on exertion and increased rescue inhaler use (4-5x/week). Admits inconsistent daily ICS use over preceding month.

VS: BP 122/78, HR 80, RR 18, SpO2 96%, Temp 36.9C, Weight 71kg. Mild expiratory wheeze at end expiration. FEV1 2.9L (down from 3.6L baseline), FEV1/FVC 69%, PEF 380 L/min. ACT score 14 (poorly controlled).
Poorly controlled asthma with recent exacerbation; likely adherence-related. Pulmonary function declined from prior visit.

Step up therapy to budesonide/formoterol (Symbicort) 80/4.5, 2 puffs BID; discontinue ICS monotherapy. Reinforced daily adherence importance. Continue albuterol PRN. Follow-up in 8-12 weeks or sooner if symptoms recur.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 4 tablets (40 mg) by mouth once daily for 5 days

coding.display: C reactive protein [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

resource.title: Emergency Department Visit - Second Asthma Exacerbation

text.div: Patient presented with acute worsening dyspnea and wheeze over several hours despite increased rescue inhaler use. Second ED visit in 3 months.
VS: BP 128/82, HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 90% RA, Temp 37.3C. Marked expiratory wheeze, prolonged expiratory phase. CRP 9.1 mg/L.
Recurrent acute asthma exacerbation despite recent step-up to ICS/LABA; concern for inadequate control or adherence.
Nebulized bronchodilators, oral prednisone 40mg daily x5 days. Improved to SpO2 95% RA, discharged. Urgent pulmonology follow-up in 48 hours recommended.

resource.conclusion: Further decline in FEV1 (2.5L, 69% of baseline) despite ICS/LABA therapy. Recurrent exacerbations suggest inadequate control; consider adherence review and add-on therapy.

text.div: Follow-up 2 days after second ED visit in 3 months. Patient admits to skipping evening Symbicort dose frequently, citing forgetfulness. Rescue inhaler use remains frequent.
VS: BP 120/76, HR 84, RR 19, SpO2 95%, Weight 73kg. FEV1 2.5L, FEV1/FVC 61%, PEF 340 L/min. ACT score 12 (poorly controlled).
Poorly controlled asthma with two ED visits in the past 6 months and progressive decline in pulmonary function despite ICS/LABA. Adherence remains a significant concern.
Intensive adherence counseling, consider adherence-monitoring inhaler. Continue Symbicort 80/4.5 BID and albuterol PRN. Recheck spirometry and ACT at next visit in 8 weeks.

coding.display: Eosinophils [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

resource.conclusion: Slight improvement in FEV1 (2.6L vs 2.5L last visit) but remains 28% below personal baseline. Persistent airflow obstruction despite ICS/LABA; adherence and add-on therapy to be reassessed.

text.div: Patient reports improved adherence to Symbicort using a phone reminder. Rescue inhaler use down to 1-2x/week. No ED visits since last follow-up.
VS: BP 122/78, HR 86, RR 18, SpO2 96%, Weight 74kg, BMI 24.2. FEV1 2.6L (up slightly from 2.5L), FEV1/FVC 63%, PEF 355 L/min. ACT score 18 (improving, not yet well controlled). Eosinophils 420/uL.
Asthma control improving with better adherence, but pulmonary function remains well below baseline (72% of pre-exacerbation FEV1). No further ED visits since step-up therapy and adherence counseling.
Continue Symbicort 80/4.5 BID and albuterol PRN. Reassess in 8 weeks; if ACT remains <20 and FEV1 does not improve further, consider biologic work-up (IgE, eosinophil-directed therapy).
